title: From Pixels to Features
concept: A raw grid of pixel numbers is hard for models to reason about directly, because it says nothing about edges, shapes, or objects — which is why computer vision builds tools to extract meaningful features from pixels.

exercises.prompt: If you handed someone a giant spreadsheet of 12 million numbers and said 'there's a cat somewhere in here,' how easy would that be to figure out?

options.text: Very hard — the numbers alone don't obviously spell out 'cat'
Trivial — numbers always show object names directly
Impossible to make easier with any extra processing

exercises.explain: Right! Raw pixel numbers don't hand over meaning for free — that's the real challenge computer vision has to solve.

cards.title: Millions of numbers, zero mention of 'dog' or 'stop sign'
cards.body: You now know an image is just a grid of pixel numbers — width times height times channels, sometimes millions of values. But nowhere in that grid does it say 'this is a dog' or 'this is a stop sign.' The raw numbers are honest data, but they're not yet meaning.

cards.title: Features are the meaningful patterns hiding inside pixels
cards.body: A feature is a useful pattern extracted from raw pixels — things like edges, corners, textures, or shapes — that's more informative than any single pixel value on its own. Going 'from pixels to features' means transforming a giant grid of raw numbers into a smaller set of clues that actually describe what's in the image.

cards.title: Like turning random letters into a story
cards.body: A page of text is just a grid of individual letters, and staring at one letter at a time tells you almost nothing. But group those letters into words, and words into sentences, and suddenly there's meaning. Pixels are like letters — features are like the words and sentences built from them.

cards.title: Why one pixel can't tell you 'this is a paw'
cards.body: Look at a single pixel inside a photo of a dog's paw — it's just a medium-brown number, indistinguishable from a pixel of tree bark or a wooden table. Only when you look at how many nearby pixels relate to each other — a curved edge here, a cluster of similar tones there — does a recognizable shape like a paw start to emerge. That relationship between neighboring pixels is exactly what features capture.

cards.title: Adding more raw pixels doesn't add understanding
cards.body: It's easy to assume a bigger, sharper image is automatically 'smarter.' But a bigger grid is just more raw numbers — it doesn't come with built-in meaning. Understanding what's in an image comes from processing those pixels in clever ways, not from simply having more of them.

cards.title: Where this unit ends and the next one begins
cards.body: Pixels are the raw material; features are the next step up.

left.text:
A grid of individual brightness/color numbers.
No sense of edges or shapes on their own.
The honest but 'dumb' starting data.

right.text:
Patterns like edges, corners, and textures.
Built by comparing neighboring pixels.
The stepping stone toward recognizing objects.

exercises.prompt: Why is a raw grid of pixel numbers hard for a model to reason about directly?

options.text: The numbers don't inherently describe edges, shapes, or objects on their own
Pixel grids are too small to contain enough information
Computers cannot store grids of numbers
Raw pixels are always corrupted or inaccurate

exercises.explain: Correct. Individual pixel values carry brightness or color info, but not shape or object meaning by themselves.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A 'feature' in computer vision is a meaningful pattern (like an edge or shape) extracted from raw pixel data.
exercises.explain: True. Features are informative patterns built from relationships between pixels, not raw values alone.

exercises.sentence: Computer vision needs a way to go from raw pixel numbers to meaningful ___ like edges and shapes.
exercises.explain: Features is correct. That's the bridge from raw data to something a model can actually reason about.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation best captures why computer vision needs more than just raw pixels?

options.text: A single pixel's value alone doesn't reveal shapes or objects; patterns across neighboring pixels are needed to find meaningful features
Raw pixels already directly label every object in an image
More pixels alone automatically produce object recognition with no further processing

exercises.explain: Exactly. Meaning comes from patterns across pixels, not from any single pixel value in isolation.

exercises.prompt: Looking back at this whole unit — pixels, channels, color depth, grayscale vs. color, and resolution — what's the natural next challenge in computer vision?

options.text: Finding ways to extract meaningful patterns (features), like edges and shapes, from the raw grid of pixel numbers
Making images bigger, since larger grids automatically understand their own content
Removing all channels from every image to simplify things
There is no next challenge — raw pixels already fully solve computer vision

exercises.explain: Exactly. Now that we know images are grids of pixel numbers, the next step is turning those numbers into meaningful features — setting up filters and edge detection ahead.
